This section walks through the complete ER design process for the Company Database — the running example that appears in every chapter from here through Chapter 8. Starting from a plain-English requirements paragraph, we apply the three-step extraction method — nouns → entities and attributes, verb phrases → relationships, quantifiers → cardinality and participation — arriving at a verified Chen-notation ER diagram.

3.7.1 Requirements

“The company has several departments. Each department has a unique name and a number. A department is managed by exactly one employee, who is called the manager. The manager has a start date for their managerial role. A department may have several locations.
Each employee has a unique social security number (SSN), a first name, middle initial, last name, date of birth, address, gender, and salary. Each employee works in exactly one department but may work on several projects. We track the number of hours per week each employee works on each project. Each employee may be supervised by at most one other employee.
Each project has a unique name, a number, and a single location. Each project is controlled by exactly one department.
Employees may have dependents. Each dependent has a name, gender, date of birth, and relationship to the employee.”




3.7.2 Step 1 — Extract Entities and Attributes
Rule: Scan the requirements for all nouns and noun phrases. For each, ask: “Does it have an independent existence and need multiple facts stored about it?” If yes → entity. If it is a single fact about something else → attribute. If it depends on another entity for its identity → weak entity.








Noun
Entity or Attribute?
Notes




Department
Strong Entity
Has name, number, locations — independent existence


Name (dept)
Simple attribute of DEPARTMENT
Unique per requirements but not the diagrammatic key


Number (dept)
Key attribute of DEPARTMENT
Underlined oval — uniquely identifies each department


Employee
Strong Entity
Has SSN, name, salary — independent existence


Manager
Role of EMPLOYEE, not a separate entity
Expressed via the 1:1 MANAGES relationship


Start_date (mgr)
Attribute of MANAGES relationship
Depends on the manager–department pairing, not on either entity alone


Locations (dept)
Multivalued attribute of DEPARTMENT
“Several locations” → double oval


SSN
Key attribute of EMPLOYEE
Underlined oval — uniquely identifies each employee


Name (emp)
Composite attribute of EMPLOYEE
Breaks into sub-attributes: Fname, Minit, Lname


Fname, Minit, Lname
Sub-attributes of Name (emp)
Component ovals under the Name composite


Bdate
Simple (stored) attribute of EMPLOYEE
Source for the derived attribute Age


Age
Derived attribute of EMPLOYEE
Dashed oval — computed from Bdate; not physically stored


Address
Simple attribute of EMPLOYEE
Single value per employee in this schema


Gender
Simple attribute of EMPLOYEE



Salary
Simple attribute of EMPLOYEE



Hours
Attribute of WORKS_ON relationship
Depends on the (Employee, Project) pairing — not on either entity alone


Project
Strong Entity
Has name, number, location


Number (proj)
Key attribute of PROJECT
Underlined oval


Name (proj)
Simple attribute of PROJECT



Location (proj)
Simple attribute of PROJECT
Single value — only one location per project


Dependent
Weak Entity
No key of its own — identified by EMPLOYEE via HAS


Name (dep)
Partial key of DEPENDENT
Dashed-underline oval — unique within one employee’s dependents


Gender, Bdate, Relationship
Simple attributes of DEPENDENT










Tip



Entity vs. attribute decision for Manager: The word “manager” is a role, not a separate kind of thing. Managers are employees who happen to manage a department. Creating a separate MANAGER entity would duplicate all employee data. The correct model is a 1:1 relationship (MANAGES) between EMPLOYEE and DEPARTMENT, with Start_date as a relationship attribute.





3.7.3 Step 2 — Extract Relationships
Rule: Scan the requirements for verb phrases. For each, identify: the two (or more) entities involved, the cardinality ratio, the participation on each side, and the official relationship name to use in the diagram.
A cardinality label answers: “Given one instance of entity A, how many instances of entity B can be associated with it?” Participation answers: “Must every instance of this entity participate — or is it optional?”










Verb phrase
Relationship
Entities
Cardinality
Participation




“works in exactly one department”
WORKS_FOR
EMPLOYEE ↔︎ DEPARTMENT
N:1
Employee: total (“exactly one”); Dept: total (design assumption — a dept with no employees would not exist)


“works on several projects”
WORKS_ON
EMPLOYEE ↔︎ PROJECT
M:N
Employee: total (design assumption); Project: total (design assumption — a project without staff is not operational)


“managed by exactly one employee”
MANAGES
DEPARTMENT ↔︎ EMPLOYEE
1:1
Dept: total (“exactly one”); Employee: partial (“manager” is a role, not all employees manage)


“controlled by exactly one department”
CONTROLS
PROJECT ↔︎ DEPARTMENT
N:1
Project: total (“exactly one”); Dept: partial (a dept may control no projects)


“may have dependents”
HAS
EMPLOYEE ↔︎ DEPENDENT
1:N
Employee: partial (“may have” = optional); Dependent: total (identifying — a dependent cannot exist without its employee owner)


“supervised by at most one other employee”
SUPERVISION
EMPLOYEE ↔︎ EMPLOYEE
1:N (recursive)
Supervisee: partial (“at most one” — some have none); Supervisor: partial (not all employees supervise anyone)









Note



On design assumptions: The requirements text directly states total participation for Employee in WORKS_FOR, Department in MANAGES, Project in CONTROLS, and Dependent in HAS. The remaining total-participation decisions — Department in WORKS_FOR, Employee in WORKS_ON, and Project in WORKS_ON — are design assumptions made explicit in the diagram. They are reasonable operational defaults but must be confirmed with stakeholders before implementation.





3.7.4 Step 3 — ER Diagram in Chen Notation
The diagram below is assembled from the entities, attributes, and relationships identified in Steps 1 and 2. Review each symbol against the legend before reading the callout.
Symbol legend:







Symbol
Meaning




Single rectangle
Strong entity


Double rectangle
Weak entity


Single diamond
Relationship


Double diamond
Identifying relationship (for weak entity)


Plain oval
Simple attribute


Double oval
Multivalued attribute


Dashed oval
Derived attribute


Underlined name in oval
Key attribute


Dashed-underlined name in oval
Partial key (weak entity)


Double line on entity–relationship edge
Total participation (every instance must participate)


Single line on entity–relationship edge
Partial participation (optional)


1 / N / M label near entity
Cardinality (maximum) on that side










Company Database — ER Diagram (Chen Notation)








NoteReading the diagram



Entities and their keys:

Employee (rectangle): key = SSN (underlined). Name is composite → Fname, Minit, Lname. Age (dashed oval) is derived from Bdate.
Department (rectangle): key = Number (underlined). Locations (double oval) is multivalued — a department can have several locations.
Project (rectangle): key = Number (underlined). Location is a single-valued attribute.
Dependent (double rectangle, weak entity): partial key = Name (dashed-underline). Identified by the combination (SSN, Name).

Relationships and cardinality:

WORKS_FOR (N:1): N employees work for 1 department. Both sides have double lines → Employee total (every employee must be in a department) and Department total (a department must have employees).
MANAGES (1:1): 1 employee manages 1 department. Start_date is a relationship attribute on MANAGES. Department side: double line (total — a department must have a manager). Employee side: single line (partial — not every employee is a manager).
WORKS_ON (M:N): M employees work on N projects. Hours is a relationship attribute on WORKS_ON. Both sides have double lines → both Employee and Project have total participation.
CONTROLS (N:1): N projects controlled by 1 department. Project side: double line (total). Department side: single line (partial — a department may control no projects).
SUPERVISION (1:N, recursive): An employee (Supervisor role, 1 side) may supervise many employees (Supervisee role, N side). Both sides: single lines (partial — not every employee supervises, and the top-level manager has no supervisor).
HAS (1:N, double diamond = identifying): 1 employee may have N dependents. Employee side: single line (partial — an employee need not have dependents). Dependent side: double line (total — every dependent record must be tied to an employee).






3.7.5 Full Constraint Summary
The table below consolidates all six relationships. The diagram column records what the drawn diagram actually shows; the source column records whether that constraint came directly from the requirements text or was an assumption made during design.










Relationship
Entities
Cardinality
Entity A side
Entity B side




WORKS_FOR
EMPLOYEE – DEPARTMENT
N:1
(1,1) total — “exactly one dept” (stated)
(1,N) total — dept must have employees (assumed)


MANAGES
EMPLOYEE – DEPARTMENT
1:1
(0,1) partial — not all employees manage (stated)
(1,1) total — every dept has a manager (stated)


WORKS_ON
EMPLOYEE – PROJECT
M:N
(1,N) total — employee must work on projects (assumed)
(1,N) total — project must have staff (assumed)


CONTROLS
PROJECT – DEPARTMENT
N:1
(1,1) total — project has exactly one dept (stated)
(0,N) partial — dept may control no projects (stated)


SUPERVISION
EMPLOYEE – EMPLOYEE
1:N
(0,1) partial — Supervisee (at most one supervisor)
(0,N) partial — Supervisor (may supervise nobody)


HAS
EMPLOYEE – DEPENDENT
1:N
(0,N) partial — employee need not have dependents (stated)
(1,1) total — dependent must have an owner (identifying)









ImportantStated vs. Assumed Constraints



Directly stated in the requirements text:







Constraint
Evidence in text




Employee total in WORKS_FOR
“works in exactly one department”


Department total in MANAGES
“managed by exactly one employee”


Project total in CONTROLS
“controlled by exactly one department”


Dependent total in HAS
Identifying relationship — dependents cannot exist without an owner


Employee partial in MANAGES
“manager” is a role — not all employees are managers


Employee partial in HAS
“may have dependents” — optional



Design assumptions (not stated, but encoded as total in this diagram):







Constraint
Design reasoning




Department total in WORKS_FOR
A department with zero employees would not exist operationally


Employee total in WORKS_ON
In this schema, employees without active project assignments are not tracked


Project total in WORKS_ON
A project with no assigned staff is not yet operational



These assumptions are explicit so they can be revisited when requirements change. To relax any one of them to partial, change the double line to a single line in the diagram and drop the NOT NULL constraint from the corresponding foreign key column in SQL.

title: 3  Entity-Relationship (ER) Modeling
text:
3.8 Min-Max Notation
Chen notation tells us whether a participation is total or partial and what the cardinality ratio is (1:1, 1:N, M:N), but it stops short of answering a frequent business question: exactly how many? “At most three active orders,” “between two and five team members,” “exactly one department chair” — these rules all collapse into Chen’s coarse labels. Min-Max notation was introduced precisely to close that gap: it keeps the same diagram shape but replaces each line-end label with a numeric pair (min, max) that pins down both bounds explicitly.
Min-Max notation (also called structural constraints notation) extends the basic Chen diagram by annotating each entity’s side of a relationship line with a pair of numbers: (min, max).

3.8.1 What Do min and max Mean?

min — the minimum number of relationship instances this entity must participate in:

min = 0 → participation is optional (equivalent to a single line in Chen)
min \geq 1 → participation is mandatory (equivalent to a double line in Chen)

max — the maximum number of relationship instances this entity can participate in:

max = 1 → at most one
max = N (or \infty) → no upper limit





(min,max) pair
English reading
Chen equivalent




(0,1)
“zero or one”
Partial, cardinality 1


(1,1)
“exactly one”
Total, cardinality 1


(0,N)
“zero or more”
Partial, cardinality N


(1,N)
“one or more”
Total, cardinality N


(4,N)
“at least four”
Total, cardinality N





3.8.2 Placement Rule
The (min, max) pair is written on the line next to the entity it constrains. The general form is:







General form of Min-Max placement on a binary relationship.



An entity of type E_1 may be related to at least min_1 and at most max_1 entities of type E_2.
Likewise, min_2 is the minimum number and max_2 is the maximum number of E_1 entities to which an E_2 entity is related.



3.8.3 Illustrative Example — EMPLOYEE and DEPARTMENT
The diagram below shows Min-Max annotation on a generic EMPLOYEE–DEPARTMENT relationship. The values here are chosen to illustrate specific constraint patterns and are independent of the Company Database worked example.







Min-Max notation on an EMPLOYEE–DEPARTMENT relationship (illustrative values).










Pair
Placed next to
Meaning




(1,1)
EMPLOYEE
Every employee is associated with exactly one department — mandatory and singular.


(4,N)
DEPARTMENT
Every department has at least 4 employees — a specific minimum business rule.









Note



The (4,N) on the DEPARTMENT side is intentionally a specific-value constraint to show that Min-Max is not limited to the four standard combinations. The Company Database uses (1,N) for this side — see the table in the next section.





3.8.4 Company Database in Min-Max Notation
The table below re-expresses every relationship from the Worked Example using (min, max) pairs, consistent with the Full Constraint Summary and the Chen diagram.










Relationship
Entity A
(min,max) on A
Entity B
(min,max) on B




WORKS_FOR
EMPLOYEE
(1,1)
DEPARTMENT
(1,N)
